posts.name: Paul Savage
posts.raw:
Brian,

This may be something like the question of who is "better", Kobe Bryant or Lebron James? :slight_smile:

I have trouble coming up with the top 10 producers in France, or even Burgundy, or Bordeaux! :slight_smile:   Maybe if you limit the list to American wines, the question is more approachable?  I have only limited access to the top California wines, living on the East Coast, and have pretty much curtailed my buying due to advancing age, stressed out credit cards, and a full cellar!  What I do have from our West tends to be "old names" like Stag's Leap Cask 23, Mayacamus, Dunn Howell Mtn,, Ridge Monte Bello, Freemark Abbey Boche, Diamond Creek, Montelena, Heitz Martha's, BV George de la Tour, Opus One, and Mondavi Reserve.  But all of this is from the '80s and '90s (and mostly singleton bottles! :frowning:  ).  Your list sounds more "up-to-date"!

posts.name: craig v
posts.raw:
1. Dauvissat - but this will change as I'm "off the list" so to speak with the price hikes
2. Mugnier - also "off this list" too, even though, I adore the wines
3. D Angerville - still buying
4. Charvin - buying for now
5. Rousseau - "off the list" too but, my all time fav producer!
6. Bouchard - mostly white Burgs
7. Chevillon - buying on a modest upswing
8. Haut Bailly - not buying anymore, too expensive
9. Roumier - not buying more, too expensive
10. Le Cadeau Vineyard

Dauvissat is my largest holding with 66 btls.  My list should change a lot in the next 3-5yrs as I start to drink down and reallocate to producers I can still afford. I have 258 produers in my cellar. Hopefully a Barbaresco producer will slip into my top 10 soon.

[quote="craig vanderah"]
Wish I had bought more Giacosa in the past. I'd love for it to be in my top 10 too.  I noticed you have Soldera and Salvioni. Do you own much Biondi Santi? Great list!

[quote="Martin Petersen"]
I'll play

1. Bruno Giacosa
2. Produttori di Barbaresco
3. Montevertine
4. Weingut Keller
5. Elio Altare
6. G. Mascarello
7. B. Mascarello
8. Soldera
9. Salvioni
10. San Giusto a Rentennano

Shows my preferences in style I guess ...

///Martin
[/quote]
[/quote]

Craig, I own one single bottle of Biondi-Santi (1985) but in general I have found these wines to lean and unbalanced for me when tasting them. Poggio di Sotto, Cerbaiona (Molinari) and Monsanto are in my Top 20 so they reflect my next level of preferences. Giacosa - I've been buying from 1996 - 2004 when released and then done some back-filling also. I'm really happy about this as I think neither price nor quality is so attractive any more ....

///Martin

3. Realm

posts.raw:
I totally agree, it's Rousseau at the top of the heap "by a long mile". Fortunately,  I was able to buy a couple mixed cases of 99,01, 05, 06  and 07 before the release prices really started to jump beyond my personal reach. It's really tough to waive goodby to a producer you adore but, no longer can afford. The '99 DRC La Tache is awesome but year in, year out Rousseau speaks to my soul more than any other wine I've ever tasted. The well of fairly priced Rousseau I once had access to, is gone.

I see a few kindred spirits here where there is overlap (including names that are high up on my list but don't quite make the top 10): [quote="salilb"] Donnhoff Bernard Baudry Eric Texier Pierre Gonon Willi Schaefer Franz Hirtzberger Noel Verset JJ Prum Alzinger Tie between Thierry Allemand and Muller-Catoir for 10th [/quote] \
[quote="Tom Maskell"] Not much Italian love on the board. •Bruno Giacosa 5.6% •Produttori del Barbaresco 3.8% •Dönnhoff 2.6% •Fratelli Brovia 2.0% •Giacomo Conterno 1.8% •Giuseppe Rinaldi 1.8% •Poderi Aldo Conterno 1.7% •Domaine Armmand Rousseau 1.5% •Domaine Ponsot 1.4% •Cappellano 1.4% [/quote] Mine looks like this: Produttori di Barbaresco (a lot of the normale/torre bottling) - 4.9% Maximim Grünhaüser (von Schubert) - 3.1% J.J. Prüm - 2.7% Brovia - 2.2% Giacomo Conterno - 2.1% Gouges (may I have a long life) - 2.1% Burlotto - 1.6% Huët - 1.6% Texier (I put my money where my mouth is, David Z ) - 1.5% Dönnhoff - 1.3% This was an interesting exercise. posts.name: John Morris
posts.raw:
A couple of thoughts scanning this:

1. Could someone cut and paste or scrape the lists and run some kind of correlation to see, say, what percentage of William Selyem owners also own X?  Eric L?  (I supposed CellarTracker could compute this over a much larger amount of data.)

2. It's interesting what I don't see much of here here:  Italians other than Barolo/Barbaresco, Spanish, Australian, Austrian.

3. A lot of recurrences of producers with VERY small production (e.g., Allemand).

4. It's amazing how many times Rhys is listed.

posts.name: Michael Davidson
posts.raw:
[quote="John Morris"]
A couple of thoughts scanning this:
3. A lot of recurrences of producers with VERY small production (e.g., Allemand).
[/quote]

For me, this is a combination of two factors. My top producer is <3%, so it doesn't take many bottles to make the list. More importantly, I drink these at a slower rate than other bottles. Allemand is 1.5% of my cellar, but 0.8% of my purchases. I churn through the other bottles much more regularly. It's probably also the case that we all have our favorite mid-range or daily producers, but we're all chasing the same trophies.

Michael

I also see that I need to find a couple of Oregon Pinot producers to buy from, as my list is very Washington heavy.

posts.raw:
It was good to see the lists of many seasoned collectors/drinkers. Over time, this board has introduced me to many European producers and my palate has changed accordingly. Many of the European producers are slowly making their way up my list (but not in top ten yet) as I stop ordering copious amounts of American wine. Ultimately, I'd like to see a few Burgundy producers, a Rhone or two, a Loire, a Rioja, A few Piedmonts, and my tried and true Americans. For now here is my list:

Anthill
Ojai
Rhys
Peay
Rivers Marie
Bodega Muga
Copain
Cameron
Cayuse
Bedrock

This will undoubtedly change, as I am off the list of 4 of the wineries here. Rising producers are Gonon, Allemand, Lopez de Heredia, Paolo Bea, Faiveley, Jadot, Roagna, Vietti.

Curious to see how this will change in 2 years, as I am feverishly (hiccup) drinking through my new world holdings to get more balance.

Fun thread!

posts.name: Bryan McKenney
posts.raw:
Thanks for pointing out the specific issues with the top producers Mike.  I know there were issues with the text and sorting and I had duplicate rows.  And this definitely manifested with producers with the most entries in the thread, skewing the totals like you pointed out.  The data was pretty hard to work with and took me quite a while to get this far.  To get any more accurate I would have had to do some manual consolidation.

But I don't care.  I just know I need to try more Ridge than the one Geyserville I had, and I need to check out Jadot, Trimbach, JJ Prum.  And access to list wines from the US like Rhys will be futile so I might as well focus elsewhere.  And I'm on the right track with Huet and Prod del Barbarresco.  I learned a lot from this thread.  It's like reading years worth of threads in one concise single thread.

I also seem to recall folks wondering at times if this board wasn't too Burgundy-centric - with Rhys, Copain, Rivers-Marie, Saxim & Carlisle all in the top 10, I don't think anyone needs to worry too much about that.
[/quote]

I think the reason for the prevalence of the U.S. producers is the fact that there are a small set of CA producers that a huge % of board members purchase, even if they don't buy broadly in CA.  For example, Rhys is at the top for me, but my cellar is less than 15% domestic overall.  The only lists I'm on are Rhys, Kutch, and Ridge, but those wines build up quickly compared with regular releases.  In Burgundy, Germany, etc., I buy very broadly and have lots of producers where I only have 1-4 bottles total.  I bet my many are in my same situation.

posts.name: Mike Stoneking
posts.raw:
My cellar started very California heavy (98%), and quite focused in Paso Robles. Over the past 5 years I have made an effort to get more diversity in the cellar and have been successful (Cali at 82%). France is now nearly 13% (175 bottles) and Italy is making a recent move at 2.5% (35 bottles). Much more diversity since the last top producers thread.

Bedrock 10.40%
Carlisle 7.40%
Saxum 6.50%
Rhys 5.60%
Villa Creek 4.40%
Linne Calodo 4.10%
Rivers-Marie 4.00%
L'Aventure 3.80%
McPrice Myers 2.70%
Epoch Estate Wines 2.40%

posts.name: Alan C h a n
posts.raw: Interesting exercise, though in my case my results are a bit misleading, both because small overall collection size means a single bargain purchase of a sixer can skew results, and because I buy from a concentrated number of producers in certain regions, but widely in others.  My most represented region is Burgundy, but I buy from a wide range of Burg producers so only one makes my top ten (Bouchard).  There is a small number of CA producers that I buy from but the mailing list minimums push my purchases higher than they might naturally go, and almost all that I buy from are in the top ten: Rhys, Arcadian, Copain, Kutch, Littorai, with Ceritas soon to be knocking on that door.  Also two German producers make my list, Donnhoff and Karthauserhof, with Aldo Conterno on the list because of last year's awesome sale.

But I consider these wines to be great values, and I have acquired them in lieu of other, more expensive, wines that I'd love to drink.  So while you guys may consider me lucky to have a lot of these wines, I am equally jealous of the folks that have listed Dujac, Roumier, etc. in their top ten.

posts.name: BuffaloLou Saad
posts.raw:
Pretty Cali centric (as expected), but starting to get more Italy and Spain.  Doesn't show much here, but more German Rieslings (beyond the Loosen), but most are 1, 2, 3 bottles per producer.

Rosenblum Cellars 4.3% Bottles (131) - Nearly all Zin
Havens Wine Cellars 2.7% Bottles (83) - Bought heavy after closing
Bodegas y Viñedos Maurodos 2.2% Bottles (68) - All San Roman Toro or Toro Prima
Tenuta Monteti 1.8% Bottles (55) - vertical of  Caburnio Toscana
Joseph Phelps 1.7% Bottles (52) - all Insignia or Cabs
Loosen Bros. 1.6% Bottles (48) - all Riesling
Cameron Hughes 1.2% Bottles (36) - mostly Havens "shiners"
Saviez 1.2% Bottles (36) - again, Zin
Torre Quarto 1.2% Bottles (36)

LOL.

The funny thing is, my meager cellar largely overlaps Matt's, yet some of these (my favorite) producers, just do not seem AFWE to me even though they are favorites of the so-called AFWE crowd.  Baudry.  Gonon.  Rougeard (new to me).  Allemand.  Roilette.  Except for perhaps the latter, these are exceptionally flavorful wines.  Roilette clearly has a leaner profile by comparison, though the Griffe du Marquis bottling has extra dimension.

That's of joke, of course!  No one in their right mind would think these (wonderful) wines actually lack flavor.  And we can agree that no one is actually anti-flavor -- people are just anti- "hedonistic gobs of jammy fruit."

That’s of joke, of course! No one in their right mind would think these (wonderful) wines actually lack flavor. And we can agree that no one is actually anti-flavor – people are just anti- “hedonistic gobs of jammy fruit.”

I get what Robert is saying, though. I drink more California wine than anything else, so my idea of “lean” probably differs from someone who drinks a lot of Burgundy. But the producers that Robert highlighted off Matt’s list are big in their own way.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.raw:
Like Sarah, my list would have been way different in 2014. My access to some wines has shifted dramatically in the intervening years, but at the top they have been that way for a long time. Chave, Allemand and Conterno would have made that list back then, but not now (I still buy Conterno but my allocations are small).

Current cellar:
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg 7.89%
Domaine Bernard Baudry 3.79%
Eric Texier 3.72%
Clos Rougeard (Foucault) 3.19%
Domaine du Collier & Fratelli Brovia 2.99%
Domaine Ghislaine Barthod 2.71%
Domaine Marquis d'Angerville & Hofgut Falkenstein & G.D. Vajra 2.19%
Hubert Lignier 2.01%
Colombera & Garella 1.91%
Domaine Louis Boillot et Fils 1.88%

Collier, d'Angerville, Falkenstein, Vajra, Colombera & Garella and Louis Boillot are all wines I've been buying in cellaring quantity since 2014 (although I have always had stray bottles of d'Angerville and Vajra).

Mugneret-Gibourg, Baudry, Texier, Clos Rougeard, Barthod, and Lignier are wines I've been buying for a long time.

I a cellar rebalancing exercise, I downloaded consumption by grape variety and then by producer. It's interesting to see what I'm actually drinking versus buying.

Consumption by producer:
2021
Azienda Agricola Caparsa	14
Flavio Roddolo	11
Hofgut Falkenstein	9
Domaine Gramenon	9
Hubert Lignier	8
Domaine Bernard Baudry	8
Tiberio	8
Henri Prudhon & Fils	7
Coudert	7
Azienda Agricola Platinetti Guido	6
Domaine de Marquiliani	6
G.D. Vajra	6
Eric Texier	6
Domaine du Collier	6

2020
Hofgut Falkenstein	25
Henri Prudhon & Fils	20
Flavio Roddolo	19
Domaine Bernard Baudry	14
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg	13
Azienda Agricola Caparsa	13
Eric Texier	13
Tiberio	12
Coudert	10
Fratelli Brovia	9
Domaine Gramenon	9

2019
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg	15
Hofgut Falkenstein	15
Vilmart & Cie	14
Eric Texier	12

Ceritas	12

2018
Eric Texier	16

Jean-Philippe Fichet	9
Ferme de la Sansonnière (Mark Angeli)	9
Jacquesson & Fils	7
Rhys	7
Le Piane	7

Also like Sarah, I posted in the Top 5 thread years ago, here is that post for historical accuracy:

[quote=", post:150, topic:114823"]
1    Domaine Bernard Baudry                          12.7%
2    Clos Rougeard (Foucault)                        10.8%
3    Rhys                                             9.7%
4    Domaine Georges Mugneret/Mugneret-Gibourg        9.7%
5    Éric Texier                                      5.2%
6    Ceritas                                          4.8%
7    Coudert                                          4.7%
8    Domaine Louis Boillot et Fils                    3.7%
9    Domaine Ghislaine Barthod / Barthod-Noëllat      3.0%
10    Fratelli Brovia                                  2.6%

This was a good exercise. The amount of Rhys that I own really sneaked up on me. Between that and Ceritas, I'm too heavy on CA pinot noir/chardonnay for my consumption patterns, I think.

I'm happy that Louis Boillot is in there and it should keep rising as I have good access to the wines at good prices (and my 2013s arrive this week). The Barthod bums me out. I just don't have access to the wines anymore at release prices, which are steep enough. I think Rougeard will move down as my allocation has dwindled over the years.

Stalwarts that would have been there a few years back, Conterno and Allemand have dwindled as the prices have soared and I've been a seller instead of a buyer.
[/quote]

So I definitely did some rebalancing of the cellar and my cellar has probably grown overall by 50% since then. The top 10 was 66.9% of my cellar then and it is only 36.7% now. That's great!

Château Branaire-Ducru 2.2%
Ridge 2.1% Bottles
Château Rauzan-Ségla 2.0%
[/quote]

Meyney is a really interesting one to have so much of. I probably should have bought more of this over the years. Several years ago, a good friend brought a bottle of 1982 to a tasting and I was really amazed at how well it had aged over the years. What recent vintages have you particularly liked?
[/quote]

Meyney is definitely a great value play - probably my favorite BDX at that price point.

The 2014 is serious juice and reminded me of Calon Segur, but with a bit more acid and a bit less brawn.  I liked the 2016 even more when it first arrived -- the complete package -- but I haven't tried it since.  Both seem built to last like the "Meyneys of old".  2012 is a classic claret with a elegant if lighter profile; not "serious" but very enjoyable.  The 2009 is also quite nice in a richer vein, but still very taut and needed more time as of 2 years ago.

The only time I've tried a 2015 it was very flat and maybe slightly corked, so I can't comment!  And I haven't had the 2010.

Meyney is definitely a great value play - probably my favorite BDX at that price point.

The 2014 is serious juice and reminded me of Calon Segur, but with a bit more acid and a bit less brawn. I liked the 2016 even more when it first arrived – the complete package – but I haven’t tried it since. Both seem built to last like the “Meyneys of old”. 2012 is a classic claret with a elegant if lighter profile; not “serious” but very enjoyable. The 2009 is also quite nice in a richer vein, but still very taut and needed more time as of 2 years ago.

The only time I’ve tried a 2015 it was very flat and maybe slightly corked, so I can’t comment! And I haven’t had the 2010.

Of course that is “lifetime” consumption. So far, in 2021 Rhys is in first. Gee, for 2020 and 2019 too. Gotta go to 2018 to find a different domaine holding spot number one. You might think it is a Burgundy or even German winery, but no - it is Vajra, which finished #2 in 2019. Fact is, I drink a lot of different Burgundy producers. Now if I sort by region, Burgundy finishes in first place for every year since I started using CT (i.e., since 2007).

Let's see what has changed in 8 years . . .

Saxum

SQN
Aubert
Cayuse
Rivers Marie
Black Sears
Outpost
Benovia
Pax (tie with Benovia)

Not much changed in style, but producers leap frog each other. Changes are interesting but not unexpected. Hospices obviously drops because there is no more. SQN shoots up because I just got on the list in 2012 or 2013. Black Sears shoots up because we (my wife more than me) just discovered it in 2012. Same with Benovia (also "her" winery). Cayuse is going up since I finally got a chance to buy The Frog and The Pig, so I buy more. OUtpost is dropping because they sold to a conglomerate. Pax is dropping because he went over to the dark side (just kidding). My guess is that Scarecrow is probably in the top 10 but I have not entered all of the boxes in CT and MacDonald is getting close to breaking into the top 10.

Not much changed in style, but producers leap frog each other. Changes are interesting but not unexpected. Hospices obviously drops because there is no more. SQN shoots up because I just got on the list in 2012 or 2013. Black Sears shoots up because we (my wife more than me) just discovered it in 2012. Same with Benovia (also “her” winery). Cayuse is going up since I finally got a chance to buy The Frog and The Pig, so I buy more. OUtpost is dropping because they sold to a conglomerate. Pax is dropping because he went over to the dark side (just kidding). My guess is that Scarecrow is probably in the top 10 but I have not entered all of the boxes in CT and MacDonald is getting close to breaking into the top 10.

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10.  The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall.  My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that's driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.
[/quote]

My list looks a bit similar. I have widely varied holdings, but, because I started buying much later than many here, with small allocations and small purchases of wines that appreciated and became scarce, numbers for those wines are low. But Burgs are 17% of my total, though the only  producers even cracking my top 30 are Jouan and Jadot. My average vintage is 2008, and fully 78.5% of my wine dates from 2010 or more recent (mostly, to me, not ready to drink at all).

Top Ten (only 13% of cellar)
Bedrock
Ridge
Musar
Cameron
Chartogne-Taillet
Dirty and Rowdy
JJ Prum
Levet
Calluna
Peay

posts.name: David Baum
posts.raw:
Dont know that Ive done this before but I kinda knew

1. Rivers-Marie - because I love everything they produce. No end to buying in sight
2. Scherrer - because I became infatuated with his zins aged after a tasting there several years ago and have been buying the futures but Im good now
3. Dehlinger - one of my early heavy buying producers, have scaled back and will enjoy drinking down. Will continue annual rose buy but can get others at retail now
4. Kanzler - my ex-GF favorite but she's gone and will be drinking over the years in her memory #RIPIslandMelodee
5. Andremily - been buying for a few years but they need so much time they just pile up for now
6. Bedrock - kinda over it, will buy selectively
7. Peay -used to buy a lot but have scaled back last few years. Really love them and their wines. Need to scale back others and buy more from them
8. Third Twin-just piling up, not sure when I'll ever drink em
9. Altesino- loaded up on 2010's when I was there in 2015 and then you know had to load up on 2015's because I was there in 2015 and saw the grapes on the vines
10. MacDonald - will continue growing albeit slowly for now. Have been on list long enough that they are starting to reach drinking windows so will drink 1 or 2 a year

Those 10 make up about 40% of the cellar. The other 60% is 117 producers

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.raw:
Top 10 by number of bottles (which represent about a quarter of my cellar)

Bruno Giacosa
Domaine Robert Arnoux / Arnoux-Lachaux
Domaine Fourrier
Domaine Robert Groffier
Littorai
Willi Schaefer
Alain Hudelot-Noellat
Joh. Jos. Prüm
G.D. Vajra
Bartolo Mascarello/Hofgut Falkenstein (tie)

Compared to what I actually drank in the last couple of years (by region/type):
German Riesling - 26%
Red Burg - 14%
Piedmont Nebbiolo - 13%
Cal Cab/Bordeaux Blends - 9%
Champagne - 7%
White Burg - 6.5%
Bordeaux - 5%
N. Rhone - 4%

I thought it was more interesting to look at it this way since what I drink tends to be extremely varied. My top two though are Willi Schaefer and JJ Prüm - and far more than any others.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.raw:
[quote=", post:63, topic:74370"]
comte-liger belair, de montille, mugnier, leroy, pernot, angerville, boisson vadot, dauvissat, chandon de briailles,de vogue.  However, there isn't that much separation between the top 10 and the 5 or six producers.
[/quote]

Wow.  It’s crazy for me to look at this now. Sold all the Liger-Belair because of the oak. Drank the Leroy. Stopped buying white burgundy and de vogue. Now it’s:

Keller
Julian Haart
Schloss Lieser
Fourrier
Prum
Rousseau
Willi Schaefer
Lauer
Jean Marc Bouley
Grunhaus

the bulk is Percarlo, as I drink the cases of CC too quickly. I do manage to save some Le Baroncole, but not enough; love opening for friends!
[/quote]

Maybe it's the CCR (?) that I'm remembering, but isn't the CC about $65?  I've a couple Percarlo squirreled-away, but have never actually tried a wine from this producer --- something I need to change.

2021 update:
1. Domaine Ghislaine Barthod
2. Domaine Michel Lafarge
3. Bartolo Mascarello
4. Cappellano
5. Giuseppe Rinaldi
6. Chartogne-Taillet
7. Domaine Fourrier
8. Prevost
9. Domaine Marquis d'Angerville
10. Clos Rougeard

Bachelet is now #18 - seems about right, they are never ready, but love them when they finally do get ready.
Gibourg is now #14 - still have a lot
Schaefer is probably #100 or so - just don't drink as much German riesling as I once did
Houillon is #20 - can't buy them anymore, but luckily I have quite a lot
Baudry is #21 - not sure what happened here, must fix that
Ganevat is around #50 - I loved the orig. J'en Veux when it was still available and still dig Marguerite and Pere.

-mark

Fun to see my wine buying and drinking patterns.  Obviously I like Chardonnay and Riesling!  But looking at it by appellation shows a different pattern.  Consumption is just within the last 3 years when I started using Cellartracker.  And I see as a region, I have a lot of Bordeaux but with multiple producers and I don't tend to drink that often.  I will have to fix that.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
[quote="Mikael_OB, post:464, topic:74370, full:true"]
Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Henrik Möbitz
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Stein
7 Daniel Bouland
8. Ca' del Baio
9. La Ca'Növa
10. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).
[/quote]

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn't have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco). 

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that's related to "b" to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son's birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos. 

1. Ronchi di Cialla
2. Milos
3. Enderle & Moll
4. Stein
5. Weiser-Künstler
6. Henrik Möbitz 
7. Elio Sandri
8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
9. Hofgut Falkenstein 
10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

Interesting to see, especially the development over time.

My top 10 (out of 280):

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Henrik Möbitz
  5. Weiser-Künstler
  6. Stein
    7 Daniel Bouland
  7. Ca’ del Baio
  8. La Ca’Növa
  9. Baudry/Dettori

Only a few bottles apart from 10 and 20 with more Italian (Barolo mainly) producers.

A bit surprised to see how may Germany entries in top 10, knew E&M as well as Möbitz would be quite high up. 6-9 are wines more recently purchased, the two Barbaresco producers plenty of 2016 bottles (birth year).

One of the main change is that white wine producers have climbed up a few spots. Intentional change, as a) didn’t have enough, to high red wine ratio in general b) my daughters birth year is 2020 and that give me even more motivation to fill up on white wines (many red have not yet been released yet like Barolo/Barbaresco).

Sandri and Burlotto has worked their way to the top 10 list as well, and that’s related to “b” to some extent as I have bought quite a few of the 2020 LNs, but also 2016s Sandri Barolos (my son’s birth year). For me the Burlotto is more near term consumption (perhaps medium and up to around 10 years I can imagine), while the Sandri LN have the stuffing for aging - I have found them to many times be better/more real with soul then many Barolos.

  1. Ronchi di Cialla
  2. Milos
  3. Enderle & Moll
  4. Stein
  5. Weiser-Künstler
  6. Henrik Möbitz
  7. Elio Sandri
  8. Comm. G.B. Burlotto
  9. Hofgut Falkenstein
  10. Weingut Keller

A few bottles bottles behind 10th are the wines that got pushed out of the list.

posts.name: Jim Hanlon
posts.raw:
[quote="Jim_Hanlon, post:388, topic:74370, full:true"]
1. Jacques Puffeney 2.6%
2. Produttori del Barbaresco 2.2%
3. JJ Prum 2.2%
3. Donnhoff 2.1%
4. Gonon 2.0%
5. Mount Eden 1.9%
6. Willi Schaefer 1.9%
7. Huet 1.8%
8. Bedrock 1.5%
9. Max Ferd. Richter 1.5%
10. Baudry 1.5%

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10.  The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall.  My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that's driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.
[/quote]

Two and half years later, my list is pretty consistent:

1.  J.J. Prum
2.  Jacques Puffeney
3.  Produttori del Barbaresco
4.  Falkenstein
5.  Gonon
6.  Donnhoff
7.  Willi Schaefer
8.  Bedrock
9.  Mount Eden
10.  Max Ferd. Richter

These 10 are about 17.5% of my cellar.  Red Burgundy is still my largest holding, even though not reflected in my top 10 domaines.  Top Burg domaines are Bize (19), Chandon de Briailles (28), Pavelot (30), Camus-Bruchon (35), and Guillemot (37).  I'm obviously value-oriented in Burgundy.

Bonus top individual wines:

1.  Gonon St. Joe (16 vintages)
2.  Mount Eden Pinot Noir (25 vintages)
3.  Clos de la Roilette Fleurie Cuvee Tardive (12 vintages)

  9. Bedrock 1.5%
  10. Max Ferd. Richter 1.5%
  11. Baudry 1.5%

Red Burgundy is actually my largest cellar holding, at about 14% of my bottles, but I buy a little more broadly there than in other regions, so no domaine makes the top 10. The largest Red Burg holding is Simon Bize, which ranks 19th overall. My average bottle age now is 10 years, which would actually be a little alarming given the size of my cellar, but that’s driven up by a number of old Nebbioli.

Two and half years later, my list is pretty consistent:

  1. J.J. Prum
  2. Jacques Puffeney
  3. Produttori del Barbaresco
  4. Falkenstein
  5. Gonon
  6. Donnhoff
  7. Willi Schaefer
  8. Bedrock
  9. Mount Eden
  10. Max Ferd. Richter

These 10 are about 17.5% of my cellar. Red Burgundy is still my largest holding, even though not reflected in my top 10 domaines. Top Burg domaines are Bize (19), Chandon de Briailles (28), Pavelot (30), Camus-Bruchon (35), and Guillemot (37). I’m obviously value-oriented in Burgundy.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.raw:
JMT Bouley, Duroche, G. Rinaldi, Cappellano, Jamet, Burlotto, Faiveley, Hudelot-Noellat, Chevillon, VCC.

I only started buying a few years ago and 7 of the 10 producers have doubled in price since I started buying them and I no longer buy some of them.

Methodology: my cellar has some chunky single vintage holdings. I looked at my largest 30 overall producers and then adjusted by stripping out the two largest vintages I own of those producers - and then re-ranked the remaining. I.E., these are producers where I have some quantity beyond just 2 vintages.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.raw:
Several differences between now and a few years ago.  I'm quite sure that Bedrock, Richter, Prüm, Schaefer, and Halcón would not have been on this list five years ago.

I have much more red wine than white, but I have spread my red purchases around a lot more than my white purchases, which explains why 4 of my top 10 are white wine producers.  

Also, with my cellar as diverse as it is, these top 10 range only from 2.3% of the total, down to 0.9%.

Finally, it's curious that my top 3 holdings by region are Bordeaux, Burgundy, and Northern Rhône, but there are no producers from any of these regions on my list of top 10 producers.  Again, that is because I spread those purchases around a lot over the years, whereas (for example) the vast majority of my zin purchases used to be Ridge and Turley and are now Ridge and Bedrock.  My top Bordeaux producer is Cantemerle, way down at number 20 overall, with Chevillon (15) and Clusel-Roch (23) taking the Burg and NR top spots.

Bedrock
Max. Ferd. Richter
Beaucastel
Ridge
Huet
JJ Prüm
Fèlsina
Willi Schaefer
Lisini
Halcón

I've also been tracking consumption since Sept. '17 in CT, and it's interesting that only 2 of these producers (Fèlsina and Bedrock) are in my top 10 of consumed bottles in that relatively small snapshot of drinking.  Daily drinkers obviously tend to cycle through the buy-store-drink process faster than many of the producers on my top 10 by inventory, and producers of longer-aging wines are going to have a lag where consumption reflects mostly who I was buying 20 years ago and inventory reflects those I've bought more recently, although the top 10 includes several I have bought continuously since getting into wine (Beaucastel, Ridge, Fèlsina, Lisini).

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Hi Ofer, 

It’s a good question, and a combination of a few different things. 

The first is that the previous 7-10 (Daniel Bouland, Ca’ del Baio, La Ca’Növa, Baudry/Dettori) are all a few bottles short of the 10th spot. That’s not due to consumption, just more of other wines coming in. 

Secondly I had more focus/priority on white wines given the red/white ratio and my daughter birth vintage being 2020. 

Thirdly, loaded up quite a bit on La Ca’Növa (and to some extent Ca’ del Baio) with 2016 being my son’s birth year. Haven’t really bought much since, found the 2017s to reflect the heat of the vintage and the Barbarescos’ felt quite tired when I tried them. I have since seen positive reviews on CT that sounds like completely different wines than I had. But I don’t rule out that they made one of those spectacular transformations only Nebbiolo can pull off…

While still good value for money, they are not the bargain the used to be before 2019 was released. For the same reason I got quite a bit of white 2020 Riesling. I’m hoping to find some interesting Barbaresco wines… Didn’t hear much yet from the tastings that took place in Langhe recently for the 2020 Barbarescos and 2019 Barolos but I guess we will start reading more soon.
posts.cooked:

Secondly I had more focus/priority on white wines given the red/white ratio and my daughter birth vintage being 2020.

Thirdly, loaded up quite a bit on La Ca’Növa (and to some extent Ca’ del Baio) with 2016 being my son’s birth year. I’m hoping to find some interesting Barbaresco wines… Didn’t hear much yet from the tastings that took place in Langhe recently for the 2020 Barbarescos and 2019 Barolos but I guess we will start reading more soon.

posts.cooked: Whites or reds(RdC)?

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.raw:
Both - of the reds I have more Schippettino than Refosco dal Peduncolo and Pignolo. As for the whites, mainly the CiallaBianco and Ribolla Gialla along with minor quantities of the sweet wines (both Verduzzo and Picolit). 

For me it’s probably one of, if not the greatest Italian wineries still kind of under the radar. I can’t think of a winery making higher quality wines with such a wide range of varieties and styles of wines. 

The CiallaBianco can be spectacular with age, had the 2005 about 3 years ago and I wrote down the following:

“Profound. Impressive complexity, elegance and structure (mouthfeel that captivates). Balanced, lively and energetic, even more so on the fourth night.

At a certain moment, having closed my eyes, it reminded of the feel of a very elegant aged nebbiolo wine, maybe because of the mouth feel together with some of the flavors I love in an aged and good Barolo/Barbaresco.”
posts.cooked:

For me it’s probably one of, if not the greatest Italian wineries still kind of under the radar. I can’t think of a winery making higher quality wines with such a wide range of varieties and styles of wines.

The CiallaBianco can be spectacular with age, had the 2005 about 3 years ago and I wrote down the following:

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.raw:
When using CT stats on value, it appears to pull from its auction/Wine Market Journal Data.

For example, the last bottle I drank was a 2007 Còlpetrone SdM.  On the page for that wine, it shows an "community average value" of $25.97, and an auction/WMJ value of $14.93.  And when I ask CT for a list of recently consumed bottles, it is the $14.93 that shows up in the "value" field for that wine.

That can also create problems for very rare bottles, like the Lisini Profillossero I had recently, where it shows a value of zero because, I assume, WMJ has no auction data.

Bouchard Père et Fils 1.5% (Corton, who knew...?)
Joseph Drouhin 1.4% (Jeez, the quality here...)

Here is how I *wish* it stacked up, knowing what I know now...
More Fourrier, Niellon and PYCM whites and a more "ticket" wines from 15'. 

Going forward?  More backfilling, fewer "daily drinkers". 
So, so many good glasses from Germany. Keep them coming. White Burgs and good Chards from Oregon? Oh yes. 
No more Marechale, sorry Frederic. And, though happy with those hedonistic le Moines, I'm probably done. 

Full speed ahead on Drouhin, Gelin and up-and-comers (Audoin, Lafouge, Corton and Marsannay/Fixin/Beaune generally) plus some ticket bottles and "curious George" samples.
posts.cooked:
Ok so. I am at 70% French, 68% Burgundy and though I blush to say it, cellar bottle #s above 2000.
Even so CT says:

Joh. Jos. Prüm 5.6% (A blast in the glass, every single, time- and so many other great producers to sip)
Louis Jadot 4.0%
Lucien Le Moine 3.3%
Jacques-Frédéric Mugnier 3.1% (99.9% Marechale)
Walter Scott 3.0% (Be told)
Domaine Henri Boillot 1.9%
Domaine Charles Audoin 1.9%
Domaine Pierre Gelin 1.5% (Be told, again…)
Bouchard Père et Fils 1.5% (Corton, who knew…?)
Joseph Drouhin 1.4% (Jeez, the quality here…)

Here is how I wish it stacked up, knowing what I know now…
More Fourrier, Niellon and PYCM whites and a more “ticket” wines from 15’.

Going forward? More backfilling, fewer “daily drinkers”.
So, so many good glasses from Germany. Keep them coming. White Burgs and good Chards from Oregon? Oh yes.
No more Marechale, sorry Frederic. And, though happy with those hedonistic le Moines, I’m probably done.

Full speed ahead on Drouhin, Gelin and up-and-comers (Audoin, Lafouge, Corton and Marsannay/Fixin/Beaune generally) plus some ticket bottles and “curious George” samples.

posts.name: Jeff Stettner
posts.raw:
In order, surprised by the Prum as I have not bought in years. I also don't really drink them, so the number is flat. Tremblay is the one moving up the list the fastest for me. 

Domaine Roulot
Weingut Keller
François Raveneau
Emmanuel Rouget
Cecile Tremblay
Château Rayas
Domaine G. Roumier / Christophe Roumier
Ridge
Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg
Joh. Jos. Prüm

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.raw:
J. J. Prum 5.8%. - Surprised: went a little nuts on the 2019s. WS and GH kabs and spats
J.M. Fourrier 4.9% - Not surprised: went a lot nuts for the Gevrey vv, aux Moines and CSJ
Jadot 4.5% - Who knew release tastings were a risk factor for buying a lot of wine?! :wow: Ursules rules. 
Jos. Drouhin 3% -  Big fan.
Le Moine 2.9%. - I get a lot of pleasure here, even if the wines are not as exalted as some domaine wines
Henri Boillot, Audoin 1.9% - Marsannay people. Be told
Trapet 1.7% - Love the 15’ Chambertin
M. Mallard, Chevillon 1.5% - Chevillon’s Cailles is a winner

Coming up from behind lately:
Berthaut Gerbet
Champagne, champagne, champagne

Wish I had more:
Hudelot-Noellat
Cecile Tremblay
